Descendant co-founded Skull and Bones at his Yale 130 years later.

his brother-in-law, a fellow Yale founder married to a Hooker sister

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended Roxbury Latin School before Harvard.

artworks.artwork_blurb: Portrait 1711 by Pierpont Limner; Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Ten ministers, forty books, one college
narrative_blocks.body: In 1700 Pierpont gathered ten ministers at Branford, each laying down volumes with the words “I give these books for the founding of a College in this Colony” — tradition credits James with six of the original forty. The charter he secured the next year became Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Two elms planted at the minister’s door
narrative_blocks.body: A poor farmer with nothing else to give carried two young elms on his shoulder and planted them before the new parsonage in 1686. Under their shade Jonathan Edwards courted Sarah Pierpont forty years on, and Whitefield preached at sixty. When the last elm finally came down, its sound trunk was sawed into blocks and scattered among the family.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brides in seven years
narrative_blocks.body: James buried two young wives in five years — Abigail, dead of a cold caught attending church in her wedding garments per the custom of the day, and Sarah, lost after childbirth. In 1698 he married Mary Hooker of Farmington, who bore eight children and outlived him by twenty-six years.

narrative_blocks.header: He drafted Connecticut’s church constitution
narrative_blocks.body: A leader of the 1708 Saybrook Synod, Pierpont is said to have drawn up the Articles of the Saybrook Platform — the system of discipline and fellowship that governed Connecticut’s churches for more than a century.

narrative_blocks.header: 1706 ordination — five ministers, all Hooker-tree
narrative_blocks.body: On November 25, 1706 he was among five ministers who ordained Rev. John Hart at East Guilford — alongside Buckingham of Saybrook, Noadiah Russell of Middletown, Samuel Russell of Branford, and Thomas Ruggles of Guilford. All five names run through the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: The household that seeded three centuries
narrative_blocks.body: The New Haven parsonage became one of America’s great seedbeds: daughter Sarah married Jonathan Edwards, and the lines that followed carried two Timothy Dwights and Theodore Dwight Woolsey to Yale’s presidency, a vice president named Burr — and, further on, a financier named John Pierpont Morgan.

education.notes: Oldest boys’ school in the colonies, founded 1645.

career.notes: Ordained July 2 1685, ending an eleven-year pulpit vacancy; served until death.
The school became Yale College in 1718.

artworks.artwork_blurb: Portrait 1711 by Pierpont Limner; Yale University Art Gallery.

Under their shade Jonathan Edwards courted Sarah Pierpont forty years on, and Whitefield preached at sixty. When the last elm finally came down, its sound trunk was sawed into blocks and scattered among the family.

narrative_blocks.body: James buried two young wives in five years — Abigail, dead of a cold caught attending church in her wedding garments per the custom of the day, and Sarah, lost after childbirth. In 1698 he married Mary Hooker of Farmington, who bore eight children and outlived him by twenty-six years.

narrative_blocks.body: A leader of the 1708 Saybrook Synod, Pierpont is said to have drawn up the Articles of the Saybrook Platform — the system of discipline and fellowship that governed Connecticut’s churches for more than a century.

narrative_blocks.header: 1706 ordination — five ministers, all Hooker-tree
narrative_blocks.body: On November 25, 1706 he was among five ministers who ordained Rev. John Hart at East Guilford — alongside Buckingham of Saybrook, Noadiah Russell of Middletown, Samuel Russell of Branford, and Thomas Ruggles of Guilford. All five names run through the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: The household that seeded three centuries
narrative_blocks.body: The New Haven parsonage became one of America’s great seedbeds: daughter Sarah married Jonathan Edwards, and the lines that followed carried two Timothy Dwights and Theodore Dwight Woolsey to Yale’s presidency, a vice president named Burr — and, further on, a financier named John Pierpont Morgan.

education.notes: Oldest boys’ school in the colonies, founded 1645.

career.notes: Ordained July 2 1685, ending an eleven-year pulpit vacancy; served until death.
The school became Yale College in 1718.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Rev. John Davenport Sr. (X02123), co-founder of New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of the man who founded New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail Davenport was a granddaughter of Rev. John Davenport, who founded the New Haven Colony in 1638. In 1691 she became the first wife of Rev. James Pierpont.

narrative_blocks.header: She died of a cold months after the wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Abigail died in February 1692, at nineteen, the year after her marriage. She was the first of James Pierpont's three wives.

cross_connections.display_label: her grandfather; founder of New Haven Colony

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Gov. John Haynes; Harvard 1658; Hartford pastor.
parents.mother_research_notes: Granddaughter of Thomas Lord, original Hartford settler.

death.cause: died in childbirth — same day Abigail was born

cross_connections.display_label: her grandfather; Connecticut's first governor

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Connecticut's first governor
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah Haynes was the daughter of Rev. Joseph Haynes, pastor of Hartford, and granddaughter of Gov. John Haynes. She married Rev. James Pierpont of New Haven in 1691.

narrative_blocks.header: She died soon after childbirth, at twenty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Sarah died on 7 October 1696, weeks after bearing her only child, Abigail. Abigail Pierpont later married Rev. Joseph Noyes of New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: She married her father’s successor
narrative_blocks.body: Orphaned of her mother at five weeks and raised by her stepmother Mary Hooker, Abigail married Rev. Joseph Noyes — the man who took her late father’s pulpit at New Haven’s First Church.

cross_connections.display_label: fathered the New Haven minister she married in 1698
was mother to the New Haven minister she married in 1698
her granddaughter, last of the line the heirloom apron passed through

notable.notable_blurb: Wife of Yale co-founder Rev. James Pierpont

artworks.artwork_blurb: Companion portrait to husband Rev. James Pierpont, 1711; Yale Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children in the house under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: Mary ran the New Haven parsonage through sixteen years of marriage and eight children, then presided over it as a widow for twenty-six more. The congregation had raised the house by voluntary subscription, and it stood for over a century.

narrative_blocks.header: Married James Pierpont the year her father died
narrative_blocks.body: Her father, the Farmington minister Samuel Hooker, died in November 1697; she married James Pierpont of New Haven the following year. The match carried her from one parsonage to the center of Connecticut’s emerging intellectual capital.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter’s courtship under the elms
narrative_blocks.body: It was Mary’s daughter Sarah whom Jonathan Edwards courted beneath the parsonage elms in 1726 — a match made in her widowed household that joined the Pierpont and Edwards lines and led on to college presidents and a vice president.

narrative_blocks.header: A son lost crossing the Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Samuel — Yale 1718, newly ordained at Lyme — drowned in March 1723 crossing the Connecticut River in a canoe with an Indian guide. His body came ashore on Fishers Island six weeks later and was buried where it was found.

narrative_blocks.header: The family that named an artist
narrative_blocks.body: The unidentified colonial painter who took her portrait around 1711 is known to art history only as the Pierpont Limner — christened for this family, whose likenesses anchor his surviving work. Her husband’s companion portrait hangs at the Yale University Art Gallery.

narrative_blocks.header: A silk apron may trace to her line
narrative_blocks.body: A rare 1730s embroidered silk apron at the Connecticut Historical Society descends through three generations of Marys beginning with her. Though scholars doubt she stitched it herself, her advantageous marriage made such a luxury possible for the daughters who followed.

bio.photo_notes: Portrait of Mrs. James Pierpont (née Mary Hooker), 1711. Wikipedia Commons.

notable.notable_blurb: Son of Yale's founding trustee; Yale tutor 1722-24

narrative_blocks.header: Yale tutor 1722-24 — then apothecary
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated from Yale in 1718 and served as a tutor there 1722-24. He then established himself as an apothecary in Boston, continuing the business until his second marriage in 1754, when he moved to New Haven and carried on the same trade there until his death in 1776.

narrative_blocks.header: His three eldest sons all married sisters
narrative_blocks.body: The eldest three sons — Evelyn, Robert, and James — all married sisters, daughters of Charles Collins of Litchfield. The Collins sisters were daughters of Charles Collins of Litchfield.

narrative_blocks.header: Whitefield preached under his elm tree — twice
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. George Whitefield was twice the guest of James Pierpont — in October 1740 and again in 1745. On the latter occasion "he preached to an immense audience from a platform under the Elm tree, in front of Mr. Pierpont's house." (Pierpont occupied his father's residence at Elm and Temple Streets, New

narrative_blocks.header: Spent his last years chasing an English inheritance
narrative_blocks.body: The last years of his life were clouded by vain efforts to obtain recognition as the rightful heir to the titles and estates of the Pierrepont family in England. (His son Evelyn carried this further — His son Evelyn pursued this claim further.

narrative_blocks.header: Sister married Edwards — the Aaron Burr legacy
narrative_blocks.body: His sister Sarah Pierpont Edwards married Jonathan Edwards, the Great Awakening theologian. Their daughter Esther married Rev. Aaron Burr Sr., and their son Aaron Burr became the 3rd Vice President of the United States and killed Alexander Hamilton in a duel in 1804. The Pierpont siblings split into two of the most consequential American lineages.

bio.bio_blurb: Yale tutor, then apothecary in Boston and New Haven

career.notes: Occupied his father’s residence at Elm and Temple Streets.

bio.notes: Also given as Ann Sherman Davenport — widow of a Davenport at time of marriage

burial.plot_notes: Fair Haven Union Cemetery, New Haven CT. GPS: 41.31140, -72.89440.

cross_connections.display_label: Friend-compatriots in the Revolutionary War.

narrative_blocks.header: Parliament refused to make him Duke because he
narrative_blocks.body: His descent from the Dukes of Kingston was formally established — but Parliament refused to recognize the claim because he was American. After the Revolution he became financially reduced and spent his final years working for wages at Yale, the institution his own family had helped found.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brothers snowbound — three sisters married
narrative_blocks.body: Three Pierpont brothers journeying through Litchfield County stopped overnight at the Collins home in South Farms when a great storm stranded them for weeks. All three married daughters of the family: Robert married Lois Collins, his brother Evelyn married her sister Rhoda, and their brother James married a third sister.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale alumnus turned Yale College employee
narrative_blocks.body: Evelyn was a Revolutionary War officer and a Yale College graduate who settled as a farmer at Litchfield Connecticut after the war. Reduced in circumstances late in life, he returned to Yale — not as alumnus but as employee — and continued in such service until his death.

death.cause: Died while visiting daughter Laura (Mrs. Col. Anson Sperry) at Plattsburgh.

burial.plot_notes: Dellwood Cemetery, Manchester, Bennington County, Vermont. GPS: 43.15250, -73.07530.

narrative_blocks.header: Three brothers snowbound — three sisters married
narrative_blocks.body: Three Pierpont brothers journeying through Litchfield County stopped overnight at the Collins home in South Farms when a great storm stranded them for weeks. All three married daughters of the family: Robert married Lois Collins, his brother Evelyn married her sister Rhoda, and their brother James married a third sister.

cross_connections.display_label: is the cousin whose son George married his daughter Sarah

narrative_blocks.header: Three brothers snowbound — three sisters married
narrative_blocks.body: Three Pierpont brothers journeying through Litchfield County stopped overnight at the Collins home in South Farms when a great storm stranded them for weeks. All three married daughters of the family: Robert married Lois Collins, his brother Evelyn married her sister Rhoda, and their brother James married a third sister.

burial.plot_notes: East Cemetery, Litchfield CT. Buried beside wife Sarah.

narrative_blocks.header: The one Pierpont brother who chose a different
narrative_blocks.body: His three brothers Evelyn, Robert, and James were snowbound at the Collins farmhouse and each married a Collins daughter; David alone married Sarah Phelps of Litchfield, settling near his brothers but taking a different path. All four brothers spent their lives within a few miles of each other in Litchfield County.

bio.bio_blurb: Lyme’s young pastor, drowned crossing the Connecticut

career.notes: Ordained Dec 10, 1722; drowned three months later.

narrative_blocks.header: Drowned three months after his ordination
narrative_blocks.body: Newly ordained pastor of Lyme, Samuel drowned on March 15, 1723, crossing the Connecticut River above Saybrook Ferry in a canoe with an Indian guide. His body came ashore on Fishers Island six weeks later and was buried at the island’s westerly end, where his gravestone still tells the whole story.

cross_connections.display_label: Both families joined through Talcott siblings a generation later.
Her daughter's marriage to Talcott converged founding lines.

institutions.institution_blurb: Father Rev. James Pierpont Sr. and father-in-law Rev. Noadiah Russell were both founding trustees of Yale in the same 1701 charter.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father and father-in-law both founded Yale
narrative_blocks.body: She was the daughter of Rev. James Pierpont Sr., one of the ten founding trustees who chartered Yale College on October 16, 1701. She married Rev. William Russell, son of Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown — who was also one of those ten founding trustees.

narrative_blocks.header: Four founding families converge in her children's generation
narrative_blocks.body: Through her parents she carried the Hooker line and the Pierpont-Yale line. Through marriage she joined the Russell-Yale line. Her daughter Mary Russell married Matthew Talcott — adding the Talcott founding line to the convergence her children embodied.

narrative_blocks.header: A hidden Hooker-Hamlin connection in her husband's family
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. William Russell's mother Mary Hamlin was the sister of Mehitable Hamlin, who married Samuel Hooker Jr. — a brother of Mary Pierpont's own mother. This made William Russell's aunt the wife of Mary's uncle, drawing a second bond between the families before their own marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Hartford's founder — nine children
narrative_blocks.body: Her grandfather was Rev. Thomas Hooker, who led the 1636 migration from Cambridge that founded Hartford. She settled in Middletown with her husband and bore nine children: Mary, Esther, William, Samuel, Noadiah, James, Sarah, Mehitable, and Hannah.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter Mary married Matthew Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Mary Russell married Matthew Talcott of Hartford — the marriage that created the Russell-Talcott convergence. Their children were both Hooker and Talcott descendants, connecting two of colonial Connecticut's founding families.

narrative_blocks.header: She may have stitched the family apron
narrative_blocks.body: Of the three Marys linked to a surviving 1730s silk apron, she is one of two likeliest embroiderers, depending on which attended a Boston finishing school and when. The apron’s Chinese silk and French metallic thread mark the family’s reach into global trade.

bio.bio_blurb: Mother of the Russell nine at Middletown

parents.mother_research_notes: Mary Hamlin Russell — her sister Mehitable Hamlin married Samuel Hooker Jr., making Rev. William Russell's aunt the wife of Mary Pierpont's maternal uncle. EH notes this double family connection explicitly.
parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown — founding trustee of Yale College

church_roles.notes: Successor to his father Rev. Noadiah Russell

cross_connections.display_label: Married the sister of his own sister's husband, both Pierpont-Russell siblings.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Yale's founding trustee Noadiah Russell
narrative_blocks.body: His father Rev. Noadiah Russell was one of the ten founding trustees of Yale College in 1701 — the same founding cohort as Rev. James Pierpont Sr (his future father-in-law). Two Yale founding-trustee families thus merged through his marriage to Mary Pierpont in 1718.

narrative_blocks.header: Double Hamlin family connection to Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Mary Hamlin's sister Mehitable Hamlin had married Samuel Hooker Jr. - brother of Mary Pierpont's mother Mary Hooker. Edward Hooker's 1909 genealogy notes this double family connection explicitly: two Hamlin sisters married into the Hooker-Pierpont line one generation apart.

narrative_blocks.header: Pastor of Middletown First Church for decades
narrative_blocks.body: Served as pastor of Middletown First Church through the mid-18th century until his death 1 June 1761 at age 70. Father of nine surviving children — five sons + four daughters — through whom the Russell name carried into the Wadsworth, Talcott, Anthony, and Dwight branches of the Hooker tree.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty-six years in the pulpit, to the day
narrative_blocks.body: Settled as Middletown’s pastor on June 1, 1715, William Russell died on June 1, 1761 — forty-six years in the same pulpit to the day.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law — both joined the Hooker-Talcott nexus.
Sister-in-law — Eunice Talcott married a Hooker descendant.
her grandmother, first of the three Marys linked to the silk apron

burial.plot_notes: Riverside Cemetery, Middletown, Middlesex County CT (per Findagrave).

narrative_blocks.header: Married Talcott two months after mother's death
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Mary Pierpont Russell died July 24, 1740. She married Matthew Talcott — son of Connecticut Governor Joseph Talcott — exactly two months later, on September 24, 1740 in Middletown.

narrative_blocks.header: Talcott double marriage with Eunice and Nathaniel
narrative_blocks.body: Her marriage to Matthew Talcott was paralleled by Matthew's sister Eunice marrying Capt. Nathaniel Hooker. The two Talcott siblings each married into the Hooker family in the same generation — one through Pierpont/Russell, one directly through the Hooker surname line.

narrative_blocks.header: Why 'Talcott' is a Hooker family middle name
narrative_blocks.body: This double Talcott-Hooker convergence — through her marriage and Eunice Talcott's — established the genealogical foundation for 'Talcott' as a middle name carried by Hooker descendants. Both lines descend from Hartford founders meeting in mid-eighteenth-century Middletown.

narrative_blocks.header: The last Mary, and the likeliest needle
narrative_blocks.body: Ending the direct maternal line with no heirs, Mary Russell Talcott is a leading candidate to have embroidered the family’s 1730s silk apron, since she would have attended finishing school in the right decade. The apron outlived the line that made it.

narrative_blocks.header: Middletown Russell-Johnson marriage of 1753
narrative_blocks.body: Married Samuel Johnson of Middletown on 9 April 1753 — son of Joseph Johnson Sr. and Elizabeth Partridge Hamlin Johnson. Their only documented child Samuel Jr (b. February 1754) died at 39 in 1793, twelve years before his father.

narrative_blocks.header: Middle daughter of Mary Pierpont's eight Russell children
narrative_blocks.body: Born February 1722 Middletown CT, third of Rev. William Russell + Mary Pierpont Russell's nine surviving children — between sister Mary Russell Talcott (1720) and brother Rev. William Russell Jr. (1725). She lived 74 years in Middletown and was buried there October 1796.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1745 tutor, then twenty-four years at Windsor
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1745, served as Berkeley scholar and Yale tutor 1749-1750, and was ordained pastor of the First Church of Windsor Connecticut in 1754. He served Windsor for twenty-four years until his death in 1775.

narrative_blocks.header: Resolved a contentious meetinghouse debate at Windsor
narrative_blocks.body: He oversaw a contentious debate at Windsor about building the new meetinghouse and found resolution. His estate at his 1775 death was inventoried at £994 including 130 books — a substantial library for a colonial pastor.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost his first wife after thirteen years
narrative_blocks.body: His first wife Abigail Andrews of Milford died June 13, 1765 after thirteen years of marriage. He remarried Abigail Newberry of Wintonbury in 1770, five years before his own death.

narrative_blocks.header: Rose from ensign to captain of Middletown militia
narrative_blocks.body: By 1763 Barbour Collection records call him 'Ensign Samuel'; by 1773, 'Capt. Samuel.' He served as a militia officer of Middletown across the decade leading up to the Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died at 41 leaving eight children
narrative_blocks.body: Ruth Whitmore Russell died April 13, 1773 in Middletown at age 41, after fifteen years of marriage and eight children. Samuel did not remarry; he raised the family alone for twenty more years before his own death in 1794.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson founded Russell & Company, China giant
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson Samuel Russell (1789-1862) founded Russell & Company in 1819 — the largest American trading house in China through 1891. Russell & Company partner Warren Delano Jr. became the maternal grandfather of Franklin Delano Roosevelt.

cross_connections.display_label: Married Gov. Talcott's granddaughter Esther Talcott in 1758

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1750 — entire ministry at one church
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1750, was ordained at the First Church of Thompson Connecticut in 1757, and served there until his death in 1795. Thirty-eight years in one parish, his entire ministerial career.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Gov. Talcott's granddaughter — Hooker-Talcott convergence
narrative_blocks.body: He married Esther Talcott in 1758 — daughter of Joseph Talcott Jr., Treasurer of the Colony of Connecticut, and granddaughter of Gov. Joseph Talcott. This Russell-Talcott marriage echoed the convergence begun by his sister Mary Russell Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: Apoplexy at Rev. Caleb Alexander's house, Mendon
narrative_blocks.body: He traveled to Boston for his health in October 1795 but was seized with apoplexy en route. He died on the evening of October 27 at the house of Rev. Caleb Alexander in Mendon, Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandson co-founded Yale's Skull and Bones
narrative_blocks.body: His son Matthew Talcott Russell named a son William Huntington Russell, who at Yale in 1832 co-founded the secret senior society Skull and Bones with Alphonso Taft. The line connects this Thompson Connecticut pulpit to the most secretive of American institutions.

cross_connections.display_label: After Sarah Russell's death, her husband Elliot Rawson named his.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Eliot Rawson at twenty-four
narrative_blocks.body: She married Eliot Rawson on June 16, 1756 in Middletown — son of Pelatiah Rawson and Hannah Hall of Dorchester. They had one child before her early death in 1764.

narrative_blocks.header: Died young leaving widower who remarried
narrative_blocks.body: She died in 1764, age thirty-two, leaving Eliot a widower with their one child. He remarried Ann Cushing of Providence and had seven more children with her.

cross_connections.display_label: He raised her future husband — and was already married to her sister

narrative_blocks.header: Her marriage united Hartford's two founding bloodlines
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Russell descended from Thomas Hooker through her mother Mary Pierpont Russell, granddaughter of Rev. James Pierpont and Mary Hooker. When she married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth in 1767 — himself a Talcott descendant through his mother Abigail Talcott — their children became the convergence of the two men who had founded Hartford together in 1636: Thomas Hooker and John Talcott.

narrative_blocks.header: The man who raised her husband had married
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable's sister Mary Russell had married Matthew Talcott of Middletown in 1740. When Rev. Daniel Wadsworth died in 1747 — leaving four-year-old Jeremiah fatherless — Matthew took the boy in and raised him. So when Mehitable married Jeremiah in 1764, she married into a household she already knew: the home of her own brother-in-law.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived her husband by thirteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Jeremiah Wadsworth died on April 30, 1804, at age 60. Mehitable lived until March 29, 1817 — another thirteen years, to age 82. She outlived her daughter Harriet, who had died in Bermuda at 23.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousins twice over before the wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband descended from Elizabeth Standley, whose sister Abigail had married John Hooker of Farmington. The Standley sisters meant the Wadsworth and Hooker lines were already kin — her marriage doubled a connection a century old.

institutions.institution_blurb: Mother of Daniel Wadsworth (HD1330), who founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.

bio.bio_blurb: Wife of the Revolution’s Commissary General

cross_connections.display_label: Married the sister of his own sister's husband, both Pierpont-Russell siblings.

bio.bio_blurb: North Haven farmer with twelve children

military_service.notes: FindAGrave: Lieut Joseph Pierpont; North Haven farmer.

narrative_blocks.header: Double sibling marriage joined Pierponts and Russells
narrative_blocks.body: He married Hannah Russell around 1725 while his sister Mary married Hannah's brother William the same year. Two siblings on each side, married into a single family in one generation.

narrative_blocks.header: His marriage joined two Yale-founding families
narrative_blocks.body: His father Rev. James Pierpont was a Yale founding trustee in 1701. His wife's father Rev. Noadiah Russell was another.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children at his North Haven farm
narrative_blocks.body: He farmed land about ten miles northeast of New Haven through the 1730s and 1740s. Several of his twelve children died in infancy, including the first James in 1727.

parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Noadiah Russell of Middletown — Yale founding trustee 1701

bio.bio_blurb: Brick maker, Episcopal founder, warden fifty years

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Royalist Episcopal church at North Haven
narrative_blocks.body: He was a founder and the first senior warden of St. John's Church of England in North Haven, elected at age thirty-two in 1761. The rector and majority of members were Royalists during the Revolution but accepted the new government afterward.

narrative_blocks.header: Senior warden for fifty years until death
narrative_blocks.body: He continued as senior warden of St. John's, with few exceptions, for fifty years — until his death in 1820 at age ninety-one. He outlived his wife Elizabeth Frost by sixteen years.

narrative_blocks.header: Brick maker who served the Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: His occupation was brick maker in North Haven. Despite his Episcopal church's Royalist majority, he served in the Revolution — a fact recorded on his gravestone in the Old Cemetery.

cross_connections.display_label: His Pierpont nephew became U.S. Attorney General under Grant

bio.bio_blurb: Yale 1751; fifty-nine years North Haven parish clerk

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1751 — fifty-nine years parish clerk
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1751 and served as parish clerk for fifty-nine years — first at the Congregational church, then at St. John's Episcopal in North Haven. The Congregational church refused to accept his resignation, so for four years he served as clerk of both simultaneously.

narrative_blocks.header: First Episcopalian appointed Justice of the Peace
narrative_blocks.body: He was the first Episcopalian appointed Justice of the Peace by the Connecticut Legislature. He served nine times in the Connecticut General Assembly, beginning in 1787 when North Haven was first incorporated as a town.

narrative_blocks.header: Lightning struck six on a sabbath-day-house bench
narrative_blocks.body: On July 29, 1771, Capt. Pierpont, his wife Lydia, and four others were struck by lightning while seated on one bench in a sabbath-day-house. Three horses and a colt were killed instantly; all six humans survived.

narrative_blocks.header: Captain in the Connecticut Militia, served the Revolution
narrative_blocks.body: He was commissioned first lieutenant of the Ninth Company, Second Regiment Connecticut Militia in 1760, and captain in 1764. His gravestone confirms he served in the Revolution.

death.notes: Died in infancy per EH; ~2yr estimated, born between siblings James (1732) and Benjamin (1735)

narrative_blocks.header: Dorothy died young
narrative_blocks.body: Dorothy died young; the dates were not recorded.

narrative_blocks.header: Fourth in his generation to marry a Blakeslee
narrative_blocks.body: His marriage to Sarah Blakeslee in October 1765 was the fourth Blakeslee match in his Pierpont generation — Elizabeth, Mehitable, and Ezra Pierpont had all married Blakeslees first. The North Haven Pierpont and Blakeslee families intertwined deeply across three generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Old/New Style calendar muddied his birth year
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker's 1909 record gives his birth as January 7, 1735, while online sources give 1734 — the difference reflects the Old Style/New Style calendar shift. Britain and the colonies adopted the Gregorian calendar in 1752, retroactively shifting January-March dates from the prior year.

cross_connections.display_label: Niece/nephew of Sarah Pierpont Edwards.
Aunt was the 'young lady in New Haven' Edwards described.
Great-granddaughter of the Yale founder Pierpont.
Brother-in-law twice over: married her sister Hannah.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Brockett brothers married two Pierpont sisters
narrative_blocks.body: She married Richard Brockett in March 1756; the year before, her sister Hannah had married Richard's brother Abel. Their children were double first cousins on both the Brockett and Pierpont sides.

narrative_blocks.header: Niece of Edwards's 'young lady in New Haven'
narrative_blocks.body: Her aunt Sarah Pierpont Edwards was the 'young lady in New Haven' Jonathan Edwards famously described in 1722, the year before he proposed marriage to her. Mary was Edwards's niece-by-marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-granddaughter of Yale's founder
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandfather Rev. James Pierpont was co-founder of Yale College in 1701 and pastor of First Church New Haven for nearly thirty years. The Brockett genealogy notes she was 'a grand-daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker' through this same Pierpont line.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at thirty-four in North Haven
narrative_blocks.body: She died at North Haven, Connecticut on June 21, 1773 at age 34. She was buried at Montowese Cemetery; her husband Richard remarried Jemima Jacobs in 1790 and lived to age 85.

cross_connections.display_label: His paternal aunt — Jonathan Edwards's wife Sarah Pierpont
His Pierpont grandnephew became U.S. Attorney General under Grant

narrative_blocks.header: Wealthiest man in North Haven in 1786
narrative_blocks.body: He was 'accounted to be the wealthiest man in North Haven, Connecticut, in 1786' at age 45, having served as a Sergeant in the Connecticut Militia. He held land that had passed through three generations: from Rev. James Pierpont to son Lt. Joseph Pierpont to him.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife outlived him by nine months
narrative_blocks.body: He died January 16, 1832 at age 90; his wife Elizabeth Cooper Pierpont died November 1, 1832 at age 88. After 66 years of marriage, she survived him by nine months.

narrative_blocks.header: His Pierpont land became 'Dr. Patten place'
narrative_blocks.body: The Pierpont tract eventually passed to his son Capt. Giles Jr., father of Munson Edward Pierpont — described by Thorpe as a distinguished jurist and diplomat. The land later became known locally as 'Dr. Patten place,' marking the end of a four-generation Pierpont landholding.

narrative_blocks.header: Named her son Talcott to mark the connection
narrative_blocks.body: In 1781 she named her seventh-known child Talcott Ives — using the Talcott family surname as his first name. This naming choice signals that her family was aware of the Talcott-Pierpont-Hooker convergence reaching back through her grandmother's generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters named Miriam — both died young
narrative_blocks.body: Two of her daughters were named Miriam — the first died at age five in 1773, the second born in 1779 lived only briefly. An unnamed infant born in 1771 also died the same year, making three child deaths in this nine-child family.

narrative_blocks.header: Moved his family from North Haven to Rowe
narrative_blocks.body: He removed his family from North Haven, Connecticut to Rowe in Franklin County, Massachusetts before December 1796 — recorded in North Haven Deeds. His son Isaac Pierpont continued the family there, dying at Rowe in 1866 at age 89.

narrative_blocks.header: Benjamin died in his first year
narrative_blocks.body: Born July 18, 1706, Benjamin died that December. His parents gave the name to a son born ten months later.

narrative_blocks.header: Died on Virgin Gorda, far from New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: The second Benjamin, named for the brother who died in infancy, graduated from Yale and died unmarried in 1733 on the island of Virgin Gorda in the West Indies — the family record does not say what carried him there.

sources.short: Life of Rev. Jonathan Edwards by Sereno Dwight

burial.plot_notes: Princeton Cemetery, Princeton, New Jersey — buried next to Jonathan Edwards

cross_connections.display_label: Died trying to bring him home from Princeton at age two.
Died trying to bring her home from Princeton at age four.
Her North Haven nephew — wealthiest man in town in 1786

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Pierpont Edwards (1710-1758), wife of Jonathan Edwards.
bio.bio_blurb: Subject of Edwards’s famous meditation at thirteen

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards wrote about her when she was twelve
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan Edwards composed a private spiritual meditation about a 'young lady in New Haven who is beloved of that Great Being who made and rules the world.' She married him five years later, at seventeen.

narrative_blocks.header: Brought home her orphaned Burr grandchildren
narrative_blocks.body: After Jonathan's death she traveled south in autumn 1758 to retrieve four-year-old Sarah Burr and two-year-old Aaron Burr Jr. She fell sick with dysentery in Philadelphia before she could complete the journey.

narrative_blocks.header: Died exactly six months after her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Jonathan died of smallpox-inoculation complications on March 22, 1758, weeks into the Princeton presidency. Sarah died of dysentery on October 2, 1758, at forty-eight.

narrative_blocks.header: Made her will the day she died
narrative_blocks.body: On October 2, 1758, in Philadelphia, she dictated her last instructions hours before dysentery took her. The two Burr grandchildren she had been escorting were placed with Dr. William Shippen.

narrative_blocks.header: Her portrait hangs in a Yale dining hall
narrative_blocks.body: She and her husband were both painted in oils, traditionally attributed to John Smybert. The portraits were bequeathed to Yale in 1939 and now hang in Jonathan Edwards College.

parents.mother_research_notes: Esther Stoddard — daughter of Rev. Solomon Stoddard of Northampton
parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Timothy Edwards of East Windsor CT — minister 60 years until death 1758 age 89

church_roles.notes: Succeeded grandfather Solomon Stoddard. Led Great Awakening revivals. Dismissed 1750 over communion controversy.

X00128.prominent_name_reason: Rev. Jonathan Edwards (1703-1758) — Yale 1720; greatest American theologian; leader of the Great Awakening; delivered "Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God" (1741); President of Princeton; grandfather of Aaron Burr Jr. (3rd U.S. Vice President). Father's father-in-law was Rev. Timothy Edwards who held one enslaved person named Ansars. Edwards himself enslaved at least six people: Venus (purchased 1731), Joab, Rose Binney, Titus (son of Joab and Rose), Joseph, Sue, and possibly Leah.

enslaved_persons.name: Venus
enslaved_persons.description: Purchased June 1731 as a young teenager. Likely later baptized and given name Leah.

enslaved_persons.name: Titus
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Son of Joab and Rose.

enslaved_persons.name: Joseph
enslaved_persons.description: Enslaved man. Married couple with Sue.

cross_connections.display_label: Giles Hooker Cowles studied theology under him after graduating Yale in 1789.
Succeeded his son-in-law as Princeton president after his sudden death.
Hosted him in final illness; published his Life as bestseller
Physician-minister and intimate friend in his New York years

education.notes: Entered 1716 at age 13. Graduated with highest honors.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1741 sermon shocked New England
narrative_blocks.body: On July 8, 1741, he delivered 'Sinners in the Hands of an Angry God' to a Connecticut congregation. The Great Awakening had been spreading from his Northampton parish for six years.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote his greatest works in frontier exile
narrative_blocks.body: After Northampton he took the Stockbridge mission preaching to Housatonnuck families through an interpreter. There he composed Freedom of the Will, The Nature of True Virtue, and The Doctrine of Original Sin.

narrative_blocks.header: Town turned on him over a midwifery book
narrative_blocks.body: In 1748 he disciplined young people in his Northampton parish for circulating what he called 'immoral books,' later identified as a midwifery manual. His own Williams and Hawley relatives led the council that dismissed him in 1750.

narrative_blocks.header: Died thirty-five days into the Princeton presidency
narrative_blocks.body: He accepted the College of New Jersey presidency in early 1758 after his son-in-law Aaron Burr Sr. died suddenly in office. A smallpox inoculation administered February 23 turned fatal on March 22.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale valedictorian at sixteen, in Latin
narrative_blocks.body: He entered Yale at twelve and graduated four years later as the youngest member of his class. He delivered the commencement valedictory address in Latin.

sources.label: Salt of the Earth — David Brainerd and Jerusha Edwards: A Colonial Love Story

cross_connections.notes: The coincidence of two daughters marrying two Ingersolls in the same period and region (Berkshire County MA) likely reflects the prominence of the Ingersoll family in that area. (DAR records; FindAGrave)
cross_connections.display_label: Daughter Lydia married Aaron Ingersoll of Berkshire

cross_connections.notes: The coincidence of two daughters marrying two Ingersolls in the same period and region (Berkshire County MA) likely reflects the prominence of the Ingersoll family in that area. (DAR records; FindAGrave)
cross_connections.display_label: Daughter Sarah married David Ingersoll of Berkshire

bio.photo_notes: Sarah Edwards Parsons (1728-1805), eldest daughter of Jonathan Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Eldest Edwards child born Sabbath afternoon, 1728
narrative_blocks.body: She was born August 25, 1728 in Northampton — 'on a Sabbath day, between 2 and 3 o'clock in the afternoon,' as her father Jonathan Edwards recorded. She was the eldest of his eleven children.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her father's attorney — Stockbridge farmer
narrative_blocks.body: In 1750 she married Elihu Parsons, a Northampton attorney who handled many of Jonathan Edwards's legal and personal affairs. Parsons later farmed in Stockbridge, where the family settled during the Mohican mission years.

narrative_blocks.header: Father warned suitor of her temper
narrative_blocks.body: When Elihu Parsons asked Jonathan Edwards for her hand, Edwards reportedly disclosed her difficult temper before quipping: 'I hope she has grace, but grace can live where you cannot.'

narrative_blocks.header: Two daughters married into the Berkshire Ingersolls
narrative_blocks.body: Two of her eleven children — both daughters — married into the Ingersoll family of Berkshire County, Massachusetts. This is a separate Ingersoll line from the Loyalist New Haven Ingersolls who descended through the Newton-Miles line.

sources.label: Salt of the Earth — David Brainerd and Jerusha Edwards: A Colonial Love Story

cross_connections.display_label: Nursed him through final tuberculosis; contracted it from him

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards called her 'the Flower of the Family'
narrative_blocks.body: She was born April 26, 1730 in Northampton — second daughter of Jonathan Edwards. After her death he wrote that she was 'generally esteemed the Flower of the Family.'

narrative_blocks.header: Brought home dying David Brainerd to nurse
narrative_blocks.body: In June 1747 she traveled to Boston to bring Rev. David Brainerd back to Northampton — the missionary to Indians in New Jersey was too ill with tuberculosis to travel alone. She nursed him at her father's home until his death October 9, 1747.

narrative_blocks.header: Contracted his TB; never smiled after
narrative_blocks.body: Unaware of the risk, she contracted tuberculosis from Brainerd. 'It is said Jerusha never smiled after Brainerd's death' — she died February 14, 1748, four months after him, age seventeen.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried beside Brainerd; Edwards wrote his biography
narrative_blocks.body: She was buried in Northampton's Bridge Street Cemetery beside David Brainerd. Her father Jonathan Edwards wrote Brainerd's biography, published in Boston in 1749 as 'An Account of the Life of the Late Reverend Mr. Brainerd.'

H00378.prominent_name_reason: Esther Edwards Burr (1732-1758) — daughter of Jonathan Edwards; wife of Aaron Burr Sr. (President of Princeton); mother of Aaron Burr Jr. (3rd U.S. Vice President, 1801-1805). Kept a journal 1754-1757, an important source for colonial women's history. Thomas Hooker's great-great-granddaughter.

yale_art_gallery_items.title: Mrs. Aaron Burr (Esther Edwards Burr) (1732-1758)
yale_art_gallery_items.credit: Bequest of Oliver Burr Jennings, B.A. 1917, in memory of Miss Annie Burr Jennings
yale_art_gallery_items.notes: Portrait of Esther Edwards Burr (1732-1758), daughter of Rev. Jonathan Edwards, wife of Princeton President Aaron Burr Sr., mother of VP Aaron Burr Jr. Esther was a notable diarist — her journal of letters to friend Sarah Prince (1754-1757) was not published in full until 1984 (Karlsen & Crumpacker). She died at 26, one year after her husband and two months after her father.

narrative_blocks.header: Colonial diarist — "The Journal of Esther Edwards Burr
narrative_blocks.detail: Esther kept a journal from October 1754 to 1757 as letters exchanged with her childhood friend Sarah Prince in Boston (addressed as "Fidelia"; Esther called "Burrissa"). Intended for Sarah alone, the letters are "startlingly frank, succinctly describing people, places, and events, and sharing both Esther's dreams and fears as well as the details of her everyday life." A 1901 publication falsely claimed to be her journal — it was a fictionalized account. The actual journal was not published in its entirety until 1984 (Karlsen and Crumpacker). Historians consider it an important source for early American women's history. On the day Aaron Burr Jr. was born (Feb 6, 1756), her only entry was: "Fryday morn. Very poorly, not able to write."
narrative_blocks.body: Her letter-journal to friend Sarah Prince, first published in full in 1984, is now a major primary source for women's lives in colonial America.

narrative_blocks.header: Princeton's first lady
narrative_blocks.detail: Esther described herself proudly as "a busy housewife." Her husband's duties frequently kept him away, leaving her to manage the household, care for young children, and host a constant stream of Princeton scholars and ministers. She relished the evenings when her husband conversed with guests about religion; she heard discussions that few women of her era would access. But the strain was immense: "I find my self much worried, feel dragged out of my senses, can hardly keep off the Bed." When a male visitor remarked women should keep conversation "to things that they understand," she recounted: "I retorted several severe things upon him before he had time to speak again... I talked him quite silent."
narrative_blocks.body: As wife of Princeton's president, she hosted a constant stream of visiting ministers and students while managing a household largely alone, often too exhausted to write.

narrative_blocks.header: The Princeton death chain: husband, father, and self
narrative_blocks.detail: In September 1757, Aaron Burr Sr. died of fever at Princeton after exhausting himself in presidential duties. Jonathan Edwards, Esther's father, was chosen as his successor and came to Princeton. On March 22, 1758, Edwards died of complications from a smallpox inoculation — he and Esther had been inoculated together. Esther, having partially recovered from the inoculation, relapsed and died April 7, 1758 — just 16 days after her father. Her mother Sarah Edwards died in October 1758. Her two children, Sally (age 4) and Aaron (age 2), were left orphaned. Young Aaron was taken briefly to the William Shippen family in Philadelphia, then placed with their 21-year-old maternal uncle Timothy Edwards (who was sometimes physically abusive). Esther was buried at Bridge Street Cemetery, Northampton, with the Edwards family.
narrative_blocks.body: Aaron Burr Sr. died Sep 1757; Jonathan Edwards succeeded him at Princeton and died Mar 1758 of smallpox inoculation; Esther died Apr 1758 — her two children orphaned at 4 and 2.

narrative_blocks.header: Diarist — her journal of letters to Sarah
narrative_blocks.detail: In the journal, Prince is "Fidelia" and Burr is "Burrissa." Burr wrote to Prince in 1754: "I have not one Sister I can write so freely to as to you the Sister of my heart." In 1757 she described a "smart combat" with a minister named Ewing who said women were "hardly capable of anything so cool and rational as friendship" — she "retorted Several Severe things upon him" and "talked him quite Silent." The journal was fictionalized by Jeremiah Rankin in 1901; not published in full until 1984 (Karlsen & Crumpacker). Portrait (oil on canvas, c.1750-1758, artist unknown) at Yale University Art Gallery, accession 1968.50.3, bequest of Oliver Burr Jennings (Yale B.A. 1917). (Karlsen & Crumpacker eds., Esther Burr's Journal, 1984; Yale Art Gallery 1968.50.3; Wikipedia on Esther Edwards Burr)
narrative_blocks.body: Esther Edwards Burr maintained a journal in the form of letters to her friend Sarah Prince (daughter of Boston minister Thomas Prince) from 1754 until her death. The letters document a woman's daily life in the late colonial period, offering remarkable insight into friendship, faith, loneliness, and intellectual self-assertion.

cross_connections.display_label: Refused his Northampton congregation, took communion twelve miles away instead.

narrative_blocks.header: Married at sixteen as Edwards left for Stockbridge
narrative_blocks.body: Her November 1750 wedding to Major Timothy Dwight came two months before Jonathan Edwards moved his family to the Stockbridge mission. The marriage was permitted because the family was about to be separated.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Yale's eighth president Timothy Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Timothy Dwight IV led Yale College from 1795 to 1817. He shaped a generation of New England intellectual life from his grandfather Edwards's old territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Refused her pew when Hooker succeeded her father
narrative_blocks.body: When the distant Thomas Hooker descendant Rev. John Hooker became Northampton's pastor after Edwards's dismissal, she placed her own chair in the vestibule below the belfry. On communion Sabbaths she rode twelve miles on a pillion behind her son to take communion at another church.

narrative_blocks.header: Heard a year later her husband had died
narrative_blocks.body: Major Timothy Dwight III died at Natchez, Mississippi in 1777 during a southern trip. The news did not reach her at Northampton until a full year after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband could carry her on his open palm
narrative_blocks.body: She was unusually small in stature and was described in old age as 'opinionated and self-willed.' Patriot rabble burned her fields and stole her livestock during the Revolution because of her husband's Tory sympathies.

narrative_blocks.header: Edwards recorded the exact hour of her birth
narrative_blocks.body: She was born August 31, 1736 in Northampton — 'between 2 and 3 o'clock in the morning,' as her father Jonathan Edwards recorded in the family Bible. She was a Northampton child of the Great Awakening years.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Jahleel Woodbridge of Stockbridge in 1764
narrative_blocks.body: In 1764 she married Jahleel Woodbridge — Princeton 1761, judge of Probate Court, Connecticut state senator, captain of Minute Men. He served in the Provincial Congress with her brother Timothy Edwards.

narrative_blocks.header: Stockbridge home base for the Edwards-Woodbridge line
narrative_blocks.body: She moved to Stockbridge with Jahleel Woodbridge — the same Mohican mission town where her father had ministered after Northampton. She died in Stockbridge in 1786, age fifty.

H00381.prominent_name_reason: Hon. Timothy Edwards (1738-1813) — Princeton 1757; merchant and judge of Stockbridge MA. His son "Edward Hooker Edwards" (b.1763) carried the Hooker name explicitly — a deliberate tribute to the founding ancestor in the 5th generation of Edwards descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: Princeton 1757 — son of Jonathan Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Princeton in 1757 and went to Elizabethtown, New Jersey to practice law. He moved to Stockbridge, Massachusetts around 1770, where he served as a judge — earning the 'Hon.' style on his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Ogden family of New Jersey
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Rhoda Ogden was sister of Aaron Ogden (Governor of New Jersey 1812-1813) and Matthias Ogden, a lieutenant colonel who marched to Quebec with Benedict Arnold in 1775. Through her, the Edwards-Hooker line connected to early New Jersey political and military leadership.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Phebe married a Hooker — double convergence
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Phebe Edwards married Rev. Asahel Hooker — both descended from Thomas Hooker, creating a double Hooker convergence in their children. Phebe's path back went through her mother Sarah Pierpont Edwards; Asahel's through the main Hartford Hooker line.

narrative_blocks.header: Married widower Eleazer Porter Jr. in 1760
narrative_blocks.body: In 1760 she married Eleazer Porter Jr. — Yale 1748, lawyer and judge in Hadley, Massachusetts. His first wife Anna Pitkin, his own first cousin, had died November 1758 leaving two young daughters; Susannah became their stepmother.

narrative_blocks.header: Her brother married her sister-in-law
narrative_blocks.body: Her brother Jonathan Edwards Jr. married her husband's sister Mary Porter in 1770. The two pairs of Edwards and Porter siblings married each other across the same Connecticut River Valley generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Son's marriage doubled the Edwards descent
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Jonathan Edwards Porter married Fidelia Dwight in the next generation. Both were grandchildren of Jonathan Edwards Sr. — a marriage that doubled the Edwards descent in their children's bloodline.

bio.bio_blurb: Theologian’s daughter who settled in Carolina

narrative_blocks.header: From Northampton parsonage to Carolina plantation
narrative_blocks.body: The theologian’s daughter married Thomas Pollock III, of the great North Carolina planter dynasty, in 1764 and moved south to New Bern. Widowed by the Revolution, she married again and lived to seventy-nine in Craven County — a long way from her father’s pulpit.

narrative_blocks.header: Among Princeton's only true abolitionists
narrative_blocks.body: In 1773 he published antislavery articles in the Connecticut Journal under the pseudonym 'Antidoulios' — Greek for 'against slavery.' His 1791 sermon 'The Injustice and Impolicy of the Slave Trade' delivered antislavery from his New Haven pulpit.

narrative_blocks.header: Mohican linguist — Washington thanked him personally
narrative_blocks.body: He grew up in Stockbridge speaking Mohican before English, writing that 'it became more familiar to me than my mother tongue.' His 1788 'Observations on the Language of the Muhhekaneew Indians' was acknowledged by George Washington and circulated through Madison to Jefferson.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife drowned at New Haven 1782
narrative_blocks.body: His first wife Mary Porter died June 10, 1782 in New Haven by drowning, leaving four children. He remarried in December 1783 to Mary Sabins, with whom he had no further children.

narrative_blocks.header: Died as president of Union College, 1801
narrative_blocks.body: In May 1799 he was chosen President of Union College in Schenectady, New York. He served less than two years, dying in office August 1, 1801, age 56 — leaving the abolitionist Edwards line for his sons to carry.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote against slavery as “Antidoulios”
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained only four years, the twenty-eight-year-old pastor took a pen name for his 1773 newspaper attack on slavery: Antidoulios, Greek for “against slavery.” He applied the golden rule directly — why are slaveholders exempt from “whatsoever ye would that men should do to you”?

education.notes: ranked first in a class of thirty-one

career.organization: White Haven (2nd Congregational) Church, New Haven

narrative_blocks.header: Elizabeth died young
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth died in 1762, aged about fifteen.

notable.notable_blurb: First US District Judge CT; Grand Master CT

narrative_blocks.header: Jefferson named him Connecticut's first federal judge
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Jefferson appointed him the first U.S. District Judge for the District of Connecticut in 1806; he served until his death twenty years later. He had earlier represented Connecticut in the Continental Congress (1787-1788) and helped ratify the U.S. Constitution at the state convention.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter married Eli Whitney
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Henrietta Frances Edwards married inventor Eli Whitney in 1817. Their daughter Henrietta Whitney Edwards later married Prof. Worthington Hooker in 1855 — reuniting the Hooker and Edwards lines through Thomas Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: His nephew Aaron Burr was six years younger
narrative_blocks.body: His older sister Esther Edwards married Rev. Aaron Burr Sr.; their son Aaron Burr Jr. became Vice President and was only six years younger than Pierpont. The two were uncle and nephew but more like brothers in age.

narrative_blocks.header: First Grand Master of Masons in Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: He was a Freemason in Hiram Lodge No. 1 in New Haven and became the first Grand Master of the Grand Lodge of Connecticut. The Grand Lodge still honors distinguished members with a medal in his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Bought Richfield Township in Ohio's Western Reserve
narrative_blocks.body: He invested £60,000 in the Connecticut Land Company in 1795 — five percent of the company — and was one of seven investors who purchased Richfield Township in Ohio. He sent his son John Stark Edwards as his first agent in the Western Reserve.

cross_connections.display_label: Newton settled the Ohio township he had bought.
Both Hooker descendants shaped the Western Reserve — land and education
He won this seat — then declined it, forcing Wadsworth back
Fellow early Connecticut lawyer-judge; husband of Edwards's niece Sally Burr.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale University Art Gallery holds his 1795 portrait by William Jennys (ART031, accession 1968.30.4).

burial.plot_notes: Center Church on the Green Churchyard, New Haven, Connecticut

bio.bio_blurb: Root of the Brooklyn Pierrepont line

narrative_blocks.header: The family genealogy runs through him
narrative_blocks.body: The youngest son died at twenty-nine, yet the Pierrepont genealogy devotes its central chapter to his line — which led to the Brooklyn Pierreponts. His widow Lydia Hemingway remarried to Theophilus Morgan.

burial.plot_notes: Indian River Cemetery / Clinton Cemetery, Clinton, Connecticut

parents.father_research_notes: Rev. Jacob Hemenway (1683-1754) of East Haven

military_service.notes: Died at Crown Point, NY — site of French fort captured by British 1759. Likely died of disease; unmarried age ~23.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven, Connecticut

H00388.prominent_name_reason: John Pierpont (1740-1805) — great-grandfather of J.P. Morgan (1837-1913). Chain: John Pierpont (H00388) → James Pierpont → John Pierpont the poet (1785-1866, abolitionist) → Juliet Pierpont × Junius Spencer Morgan → John Pierpont Morgan. J.P. Morgan's middle name "Pierpont" commemorates this descent from the Pierpont family of New Haven — founded by Rev. James Pierpont, who married Mary Hooker (H00034). Thomas Hooker's great-great-great-granddaughter's grandson became the most powerful financier in American history.

bio.notes: Sources also give surname as "Pierrepont" — alternate spelling

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandfather of J.P. Morgan
narrative_blocks.body: His great-grandson was John Pierpont Morgan (1837-1913), the financier whose name 'J.P. Morgan' carries forward this Pierpont line. Through his son James Pierpont and grandson John Pierpont the poet, the name traveled four generations to one of America's most consequential bankers.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived in old Pierpont mansion, New Haven
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Dwight described him as 'a large landholder who lived in the old family-mansion at New Haven.' He had ten children, of whom only four reached adulthood and only three married.

narrative_blocks.header: Family later restored 'Pierrepont' French spelling
narrative_blocks.body: He was born 'Pierpont' but late in his life, around 1802, Hezekiah Beers Pierpont requested the family restore the original French 'Pierrepont' spelling. Hezekiah did not adopt it himself for business reasons; descendants split between the two spellings thereafter.

career.notes: Also selectman, town officer, and magistrate.

narrative_blocks.header: He fortified his mills in Philip’s War
narrative_blocks.body: During King Philip’s War in 1675-6, John fortified his Roxbury homestead, “where were malting and mills for grinding and fulling, God having blessed him with a considerable estate.” The tide-mill’s foundation timbers were still visible two centuries later.

narrative_blocks.header: A son who carried Roxbury to Carolina
narrative_blocks.body: His son Benjamin led a company from Roxbury in 1691 to found an independent church in South Carolina, and died in the ministry at Charleston — while James, the son who stayed, founded a college in Connecticut.

cross_connections.display_label: married his son James, the minister who founded Yale

narrative_blocks.header: She died when James was five
narrative_blocks.body: Thankful died at thirty-five, when her son James — the future founder of Yale — was five years old. Her father, Deacon John Stow, had given the land that seated the Pierponts in Roxbury.

cross_connections.display_label: married her son James, the minister who founded Yale

burialCemetery.name: Center Church on the Green Churchyard
alt_names: First Church of Christ Burial Ground
burialCemetery.notes: Historic underground crypt beneath Center Church on the New Haven Green, with associated outdoor burial ground. Burial place of Martha Newton Whittlesey (H00143) and Elizabeth Susanna Whittlesey Barnes (HD0101).

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.

INST073.name: Roxbury Latin School
INST073.notes: Oldest school in continuous operation in North America, founded 1645. Rev. James Pierpont (X00126) attended before Harvard College.

crossConnections.display_label: Both founding trustees of Yale 1701; Pierson was first rector.
Pierpont built the institution Davenport had envisioned.
Descendant co-founded Skull and Bones at his Yale 130 years later.
coined the Ivy League name for colleges like Yale
his brother-in-law, a fellow Yale founder married to a Hooker sister
